name: Liu et al. LLM Respondent Model and Ensemble Effects Extract
population_or_context: LLM respondents generating College Algebra item answers compared with undergraduate human respondents.
sample_size: The arXiv full text reports 20 OpenStax College Algebra items, 150 responses per LLM, six LLMs, and human item-response data with effectively N >= 99 for all items.
methods: Extracted model-choice and ensemble/resampling effects from the LLM respondent psychometric analysis.
education_context: College Algebra multiple-choice item evaluation using OpenStax items and undergraduate respondent data.
response_origin: Synthetic LLM respondents from multiple models are compared with human undergraduate responses.
rubric_label_space: Correct/incorrect College Algebra item responses calibrated with Rasch/IRT methods.
label_source: Human response data and manually graded LLM responses provide correctness data for IRT calibration.
generation_model: GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Llama 2, Llama 3, Gemini-Pro, and Cohere Command R Plus.
generation_method: Each model generates a pool of LLM respondent answers to the same item set; augmentation experiments resample and mix synthetic respondents with human responses.
prompt_strategy: A simple uniform prompt presents 20 labeled College Algebra questions; the paper notes that item-by-item and model-customized prompting remain future work.
evaluation_method: Fixed-parameter IRT and separate calibrations compare human and LLM respondent proficiency distributions and item-parameter correlations.
scorer_use: Item-evaluation and item-calibration support; not automated scorer validation.
real_response_comparator: Human undergraduate responses to the same College Algebra items provide the comparator.
reported_metrics: Table 1 reports human SD 0.98 versus LLM SDs 0.29 to 0.58; GPT-3.5 mean 0.27 SD 0.58, GPT-4 mean 0.00 SD 0.31, Llama 2 mean -1.81 SD 0.44, and Llama 3 mean 0.37 SD 0.51. Table 2 reports item-calibration correlations including GPT-3.5 Pearson 0.83/Spearman 0.87, GPT-4 Pearson 0.72/Spearman 0.78, and Experiment 3 Pearson 0.93/Spearman 0.93.
limitations: The item set is a single College Algebra lesson, so model effects may not generalize to other subjects, levels, or response formats.
The authors report that no single LLM fully mimics human respondent variability because LLM proficiency distributions are narrower.
The best augmentation strategy improves rank correlation but also changes RMSE, so ensemble results are mixed rather than a replacement for human data.
